exercises.title: Why every audio model uses a neural codec
exercises.prompt: A 5-second clip at 16-48 kHz is 80-240k raw samples. Explain why every production audio generator in 2026 first compresses audio to discrete tokens with a neural codec (Encodec, SoundStream, DAC), and what the codec's encoder and decoder do.
exercises.hint: No transformer attends to 240k continuous samples. Codec encoder → ~50-75 Hz discrete tokens; model generates tokens; decoder → waveform. Same idea as the VAE for images / spacetime patches for video.
exercises.solution:
**The problem:** raw audio is a **continuous 1-D signal at 16-48 kHz**, so even a 5-second clip is **80-240k samples**. No transformer can attend to a sequence that long (quadratic attention dies), and continuous samples don't fit the discrete next-token paradigm. Modeling waveforms directly is infeasible for generation.

**The universal fix — a neural codec:** 
- The **encoder** compresses the waveform into a **short sequence of discrete tokens** at a low frame rate (~**50-75 Hz** instead of 16-48 kHz) drawn from a learned codebook — typically via **residual vector quantization** (RVQ): multiple codebooks, each refining the previous one's residual (Phase 6.13).
- A **transformer or diffusion model then generates those tokens** (autoregressively or by denoising), exactly like generating text tokens.
- The **decoder** reconstructs a **high-fidelity waveform** from the generated tokens.

**Why it works / what it parallels:** the codec turns 'model 240k continuous samples' into 'model a few hundred discrete tokens' — a tractable sequence-modeling problem. It's the **same compression-then-generate-in-latent idea** as the **VAE for latent diffusion** (images, Lesson 7) and **spacetime patches for video** (Lesson 10): offload perceptual detail to a learned codec/VAE, do the expensive generative modeling in the compact latent, then decode. Encodec, SoundStream, and DAC are the audio equivalents of SD's VAE — and the **semantic-vs-acoustic token split** (Phase 6.13) makes them even better suited to generation.

exercises.title: Three tasks, two paradigms
exercises.prompt: Audio generation splits into TTS, music, and sound effects, and uses two main paradigms: autoregressive transformers over codec tokens (VALL-E, MusicGen) and diffusion (Stable Audio, AudioLDM). Explain which paradigm suits which task and why, and what makes TTS structurally easier than open-domain music.
exercises.hint: AR-over-tokens: strong for structured/sequential (speech, controllable music). Diffusion: strong for broad continuous audio (music, SFX), parallel sampling. TTS = narrow, strong phonetic structure, alignment to text; music = much broader distribution, long-range structure.
exercises.solution:
**The three tasks:**
- **Text-to-speech (TTS):** text → speech (VALL-E, NaturalSpeech 3, ElevenLabs, OpenAI TTS).
- **Music generation:** prompt (text/melody/genre) → music (MusicGen, Stable Audio 2.5, Suno, Udio, Riffusion).
- **Sound effects / Foley:** prompt → ambient/sound design (AudioGen, AudioLDM 2, Stable Audio Open).

**Two paradigms and their fit:**
- **Autoregressive transformer over codec tokens** (VALL-E, MusicGen): predicts audio tokens one (frame) at a time, like an LLM. Strong where the signal is **sequential and structured**, where you want **streaming** and **strong conditioning/continuation** (e.g. continue a melody, clone a voice from a 3-s prompt). VALL-E treats TTS as 'language modeling' over codec tokens; MusicGen does controllable music this way. Downsides: serial sampling, error accumulation over long clips.
- **Diffusion** (Stable Audio, AudioLDM 2): denoises a latent audio representation. Strong for **broad, continuous distributions** (music, SFX) where **mode coverage and global structure** matter, and it samples **in parallel per step** (no token-by-token serialization). Often operates on a latent spectrogram or codec latent. Stable Audio uses latent diffusion with timing conditioning for full-length music.

**Why TTS is structurally easier than open-domain music:**
1. **Narrow distribution.** Clean speech is **narrow-band and highly structured** — a limited set of phonemes, prosody patterns, and a single speaker at a time. Music spans a **vastly broader distribution** (any instrument, genre, arrangement, polyphony), so the model must cover far more variety.
2. **Strong phonetic structure + tight text alignment.** Speech has an almost **deterministic mapping from text to sound** (phonemes → audio), giving a strong, learnable signal and clear alignment between input text and output. Music has **no such tight conditioning** — a prompt like 'epic orchestral' permits millions of valid outputs with **long-range structure** (rhythm, harmony, song form over minutes) that's hard to keep coherent.
3. **Shorter coherence horizon.** A spoken utterance needs consistency over seconds; a song needs **musical structure over minutes** (verse/chorus, key, tempo), a much harder long-range modeling problem.
So TTS was 'solved well' early by AR-over-tokens (narrow, aligned, structured), while **open-domain music** remains harder and benefits from diffusion's coverage (or hybrid AR+diffusion), with quality still improving fast in 2026.

exercises.title: Long-range structure, real-time, and the hybrid future
exercises.prompt: Audio generation faces tensions between quality, long-range musical structure, latency (real-time/streaming), and controllability. Explain how the semantic/acoustic token split helps, the AR-vs-diffusion tradeoff for these axes, how real-time/full-duplex needs differ (relate to Phase 6 Moshi/Hibiki), and where hybrid systems are heading.
exercises.hint: Semantic tokens (content) + acoustic tokens (timbre) let a model plan structure then render. AR = streaming/low-latency but serial; diffusion = quality/parallel but offline. Real-time speech (Moshi) needs token streaming, full-duplex. Hybrid: AR for structure + diffusion/codec for fidelity.
exercises.solution:
**The semantic/acoustic token split helps with structure.** Modern codecs (Mimi, and the SoundStream/Encodec lineage) separate **semantic tokens** (content — *what* is said/played, phonetic/musical structure) from **acoustic tokens** (timbre, speaker identity, fine texture — *how* it sounds) (Phase 6.13). For generation this is powerful: the model can **plan the high-level structure in semantic tokens** (the melody, the words, the form) and then **render fidelity in acoustic tokens** — decoupling 'compose/say the right thing' from 'make it sound good'. This eases **long-range coherence** (reason over compact semantic content) and enables **voice/style control** (swap acoustic tokens, keep content). It's the audio analog of separating layout from texture in images.

**AR vs diffusion across the axes:**
- **Latency / streaming:** **AR-over-tokens wins** — it generates tokens **incrementally**, so you can **stream** audio out as it's produced (essential for real-time). Diffusion typically needs the **whole clip's** multi-step denoising before output (offline), though fast samplers/consistency models and chunked diffusion are closing this.
- **Quality / coverage / global structure:** **diffusion often wins** on broad music/SFX (mode coverage, parallel-per-step, fewer accumulation errors) and full-length musical structure with timing conditioning.
- **Long clips:** AR **accumulates errors/drifts** over long sequences (like long video, Lesson 10); diffusion handles a fixed window coherently but extending it is its own problem.
- **Controllability:** AR gives natural **continuation/conditioning** (prompt-then-continue, voice cloning); diffusion gives strong **prompt/CFG and inpainting-style edits** (regenerate a section of music).

**Real-time / full-duplex is a different regime (Phase 6.15, Moshi/Hibiki):** conversational speech needs **~200 ms latency, streaming, and full-duplex** (listen while speaking) — which the classic ASR→LLM→TTS pipeline can't hit. Moshi/Hibiki model **parallel audio token streams** (user + assistant) over **Mimi codec tokens** in **one model**, generating acoustic tokens directly and continuously. That's fundamentally an **AR-over-codec-tokens, streaming** design — diffusion's offline multi-step sampling is the wrong tool for live dialogue. Real-time TTS similarly favors **streaming AR** (or distilled few-step generation).

**Where hybrids are heading:** the field is converging on **combining the strengths**:
- **AR for structure + diffusion/codec-decoder for fidelity:** an AR model predicts **semantic/coarse tokens** (structure, content, low latency, streamable) and a **diffusion or flow-matching decoder** renders **high-fidelity acoustic detail** (quality) — e.g. semantic-token LM → diffusion vocoder. You get streaming structure *and* diffusion-quality audio.
- **Flow matching** (Lesson 13) to cut diffusion's step count toward real-time.
- **Unified token spaces** so speech, music, and SFX share a codec and a backbone, and so audio generation slots into multimodal LLMs (audio-in/audio-out).
- **Distillation/consistency** to make diffusion audio few-step and thus viable for low latency.
The synthesis mirrors the whole phase: **compress to tokens (codec) → model the compact representation (AR and/or diffusion, increasingly both) → decode to high fidelity**, with the AR-vs-diffusion choice driven by the **latency-vs-quality-vs-length** demands of the specific task — and the frontier blending them so you no longer have to choose.
